title: What Is Worship?
subtitle: Devotion to God Alone in Islam, and the Question of Worshipping Jesus in Christianity
summary: Worship, ibadah in Arabic, means directing ultimate devotion, submission, and love toward God, and Islam treats it as the very purpose of human existence. Islamic worship is deliberately comprehensive, covering ritual prayer and charity as well as everyday acts of sincerity, but it carries one non-negotiable condition: it must be for God alone, never shared with any created being. Mainstream Christianity also insists it worships only the one God, yet historic Christian doctrine extends that worship to Jesus Christ and the Holy Spirit as the same God in three persons. This article explains both positions from their own sources, quotes the Quran and the Bible directly, and lays out the Islamic response to the Christian case for worshipping Jesus.

seo.metaTitle: What Is Worship? Islam's Exclusive Devotion to God vs Worship of Jesus
seo.metaDescription: A beginner's guide to worship (ibadah) in Islam as devotion to God alone, the Christian case for worshipping Jesus from John and Philippians, and the Islamic response.
keywords: John 20:28 my Lord and my God

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Worship is one of those words everyone thinks they understand until asked to define it. In Islam, the Arabic term is ibadah, and it names something both simpler and larger than a single ritual: the total submission of a person's heart, words, and actions to God, done because God alone deserves them.

Muslims believe worship is the very reason human beings and jinn (a class of unseen beings mentioned in the Quran) exist. It includes obvious rituals like the five daily prayers, fasting, and charity, but also ordinary life lived with sincere intention: honest work, kindness to parents, even a smile. The one requirement that never bends is exclusivity: worship belongs to God alone, never divided with a prophet, an angel, or anyone else.

Christianity agrees that worship belongs to God alone, and insists it has never abandoned that principle. Yet mainstream Christian doctrine also directs worship toward Jesus Christ and the Holy Spirit, understanding them as the same one God known in three persons. This article explains the Islamic concept of worship in full, quotes the Quran and the Bible directly on the subject, presents the Christian reasoning behind worshipping Jesus fairly, and then sets out how Islam responds.

sections.title: Introduction: A Word Larger Than "Prayer"
sections.body:
The Arabic word for worship, ibadah, comes from a root meaning "to serve" or "to submit." Muslim scholars have long taught that ibadah is a far wider concept than the English word "worship" might suggest to a newcomer. It is not confined to the five daily prayers (salah), fasting during Ramadan, giving charity (zakah), or the pilgrimage to Mecca (hajj), even though those major acts of devotion sit at its center.

Ibadah also names something broader: any word or deed that God loves and is pleased with, whether an outward ritual or a hidden intention of the heart. A person who works honestly, speaks a kind word, removes something harmful from a public path, or simply feels grateful to God is engaged in worship, provided the act is sincere. Narrations in the hadith literature, the recorded traditions of the Prophet Muhammad, describe him teaching his companions that even a smile shared with another person can count as an act of devotion when the intention behind it is right.

Worship in Islam therefore touches the whole of life rather than a separate compartment visited on holy days. What unifies it all, from a five-minute prayer to a decade of honest labor, is one non-negotiable condition: it must be for God, and for God alone.

sections.title: The Question: Can Worship Ever Be Shared?
sections.body:
The question this article addresses touches the center of both religions: who, or what, deserves worship, and can worship ever be divided? Islam answers with total clarity: worship (ibadah) belongs to God alone. This is the second great pillar of Tawhid, the Islamic doctrine of God's oneness discussed elsewhere in this library: not merely that God is one in number, but that He alone has the right to receive prayer, sacrifice, love, and submission. No prophet, angel, or righteous person may receive even a fraction of what belongs to God. Directing any act of worship toward someone or something other than God is called shirk, associating partners with God, treated as the gravest of all wrongs because it gives away the one thing that belongs to God alone.

Christianity answers the same question differently, though it also insists it is affirming, not abandoning, monotheism. Mainstream Christian doctrine holds that God exists eternally as three persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, together the one God, not three gods. On that basis, worship offered to Jesus Christ, understood as God the Son, is regarded as worship of that same one God, not a second object of devotion beside the Father. This article presents that reasoning fairly, in its own terms, before turning to how Islam responds.

sections.title: From the Earliest Church to Seventh-Century Arabia
sections.body:
The earliest Christian communities, in the decades following Jesus's earthly ministry, wrestled with the question of his exact relationship to God. The Gospels record moments, examined below, in which the risen Jesus is addressed in exalted terms by his followers. Over the following centuries, church councils produced increasingly precise doctrinal formulas. The Council of Nicaea in 325 and the Council of Constantinople in 381 issued creeds affirming the full divinity of the Son and the Holy Spirit alongside the Father, three persons sharing one divine essence. From that point forward, mainstream Christian liturgy has directed worship to the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit together.

Islam arrived in seventh-century Arabia into a religious landscape that already included this developed Trinitarian Christianity, alongside Arabian polytheism and Jewish monotheism. The Quran's response to both was the same: worship belongs to God alone, without partner, offspring, or any created being sharing in it, however revered. The Quran honors Jesus as a mighty messenger, born of a virgin, who spoke in his mother's defense as an infant and performed miracles by God's permission, while insisting he himself was a devoted worshipper of God, not a proper object of worship alongside Him. That is the fork this article examines: the same Jesus, read by one tradition as included in the object of worship, and by the other as among worship's most exemplary practitioners.

sections.title: Worship in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran states the purpose behind human and jinn existence in a single, direct verse:

وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ

"And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me." (Quran 51:56)

Worship, on this verse, is not one activity among many a believer might choose; it is the reason for existing at all.

That same exclusivity is recited by Muslims many times a day, in every unit of the five daily prayers, through the opening chapter of the Quran, Surah al-Fatiha:

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ

"It is You we worship and You we ask for help." (Quran 1:5)

The Arabic word order places the object, "You," before the verb, a construction used for emphasis: it is You, and only You, whom we worship. This single line, repeated by well over a billion people daily, is perhaps the most recited assertion of exclusive worship in human history.

The Quran ties this same exclusivity directly to one of life's most important relationships, that between a child and aging parents:

۞ وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَٰنًا ۚ إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّۢ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًۭا كَرِيمًۭا

"And your Lord has decreed that you not worship except Him, and to parents, good treatment. Whether one or both of them reach old age [while] with you, say not to them [so much as], 'uff,' and do not repel them but speak to them a noble word." (Quran 17:23)

The pairing is deliberate: worship of God and kindness to parents sit side by side but are never confused; parents receive honor, God alone receives worship. Islam can hold a created being in the highest human esteem without transferring to that being what belongs only to God.

Finally, the Quran describes what sincere worship looks like in practice, combining inward intention with outward ritual:

وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ حُنَفَآءَ وَيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ دِينُ ٱلْقَيِّمَةِ

"And they were not commanded except to worship Allah, [being] sincere to Him in religion, inclining to truth, and to establish prayer and to give zakah. And that is the correct religion." (Quran 98:5)

Worship here is not reduced to inward feeling alone, nor to outward ritual alone; it requires both sincerity (ikhlas) and action, expressed through prayer and charity. Muslim scholars describe ikhlas as the condition separating worship from mere performance: an act counts as worship only when aimed at God alone, with no admixture of showing off or seeking any other reward.

sections.title: Worship in the Bible: The Case for Worshipping Jesus
sections.body:
The Bible itself offers a definition of true worship in words attributed to Jesus during his conversation with a Samaritan woman at a well. Explaining that worship no longer depends on any single holy mountain or temple, Jesus tells her:

"God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth." (John 4:24)

Read on its own, this verse describes worship as an inward, spiritual reality directed toward God the Father, whom Jesus calls "him," distinguishing the object of worship from himself as the speaker.

Yet other passages are read by Christians as extending worship to Jesus himself. The clearest is the account of the disciple Thomas, who had doubted the resurrection until he saw the risen Jesus with his own eyes. John's Gospel records his response:

"Thomas answered him, "My Lord and my God!"" (John 20:28)

Many Christian theologians read this as a direct, personal confession of Jesus's divinity, made to his face, and note that the Gospel does not portray Jesus correcting or rebuking Thomas for it, unlike scenes elsewhere in scripture where a human figure or angel deflects worship intended for God alone. For Christians, that Jesus receives the confession without correction is significant evidence that early Christian memory understood him as rightly addressed this way.

The apostle Paul's letter to the Philippians is read the same way, and in even more explicitly worshipful language. Paul writes that God exalted Jesus to the highest place, so:

"that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven, those on earth, and those under the earth, and that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." (Philippians 2:10-11)

Christian scholars widely note that this language echoes a declaration in Isaiah where God announces that every knee will bow to him alone. Applying that language to Jesus, Paul is read as placing him within the identity of the one God who receives such universal homage, while still framing the event as bringing glory "to God the Father," not competing with him.

Alongside these texts stands a very different statement, from earlier in Jesus's ministry. Tempted by Satan to bow down to him, Jesus responds by quoting the Hebrew scriptures directly:

"Then Jesus said to him, "Get behind me, Satan! For it is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.'"" (Matthew 4:10)

Taken together, these four passages show the Bible's full range on worship: worship as spiritual devotion to God (John 4:24); two texts read as extending worship to Jesus as God the Son (John 20:28 and Philippians 2:10-11); and a statement from Jesus that worship belongs to God alone (Matthew 4:10). Mainstream Christian theology holds all four together without contradiction, understanding Jesus, as God the Son, to be included within the very "God" that Matthew 4:10 commands worship toward, not a rival to Him.

sections.title: Side by Side: Whom Do We Worship?
sections.body:
It helps to state each position honestly, in full, before comparing them.

Islam holds that worship is God's exclusive and undivided right. Every act of ibadah, from the five daily prayers to a sincere kindness, must be directed to God alone; nothing may be given even a share of it, including the greatest of His prophets. On this view, Jesus was a mighty messenger who worshipped God perfectly, as Matthew 4:10 itself records him insisting, and devotion belongs to the God Jesus worshipped, not to Jesus as a second object of devotion.

Mainstream Christianity holds that God is one being in three co-equal, co-eternal persons, and that worship offered to the Son and the Holy Spirit is worship of that same one God, not a second or third deity. On this view, Thomas's confession in John 20:28 and Paul's hymn in Philippians 2:10-11 are not additions to monotheism but its fullest expression, because the one worshipped, in the end, is the one God who eternally exists as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

The Islamic response engages this reasoning on its own terms. First, on Thomas's exclamation, both halves of the sentence need an answer, not only the first. "My Lord" alone would be the easier half: calling a revered human figure "my lord" in a moment of shock has real precedent in Semitic speech as an honorific, the cry of a startled disciple rather than a claim of deity, much as the Hebrew word for "lord," adon, is applied to kings, husbands, and other men throughout the Hebrew Bible without implying they are God. "My God" is the harder half, and Muslims recognize it does not have that same easy secular parallel; it cannot simply be set aside or answered by discussing "my Lord" alone. The Islamic reading takes the whole sentence as a single exclamation: an astonished invocation of God's own name and power, uttered in the very moment Thomas is confronted with what he takes to be God's unmistakable act of raising Jesus from the dead, in the way a startled monotheist might cry out using God's own title without thereby asserting that the man now standing in front of him is literally God in the flesh. That reading is reinforced by what John's Gospel does not narrate immediately after the words: no bowing, no prostration, no rite of the kind scripture elsewhere associates with formal worship of God, only a spoken exclamation. Muslims argue that an exclamation, however striking, and even granting that it names God directly, remains a thin foundation on its own for a doctrine that reshapes the meaning of God's oneness, particularly set against the Bible's own explicit statement in Matthew 4:10 that worship belongs to God alone.

Second, on Paul's hymn: the passage states that the outcome of every knee bowing is glory "to God the Father," not to Jesus in isolation. Muslims read this as consistent with their view of Jesus: a servant so honored that God exalts his name above every other name, yet the honor still flows upward to the Father rather than resting in the Son. The verses just before the quoted lines state that God "highly exalted" Jesus and "gave to him" that name, language of one being bestowing honor on another, which Muslims see as fitting an exalted messenger more than a self-existing deity who could receive nothing from anyone.

Third, and most direct: Matthew 4:10 records Jesus's own words that worship and service belong to "the Lord your God" alone. Muslims ask: if Jesus intended his followers to eventually worship him, why would the Gospel preserve, without qualification, his own statement that worship belongs to God alone? Islam's answer is that the statement should be read at face value: Jesus, like every prophet before him, called people to worship the one God, and it is that call, Muslims believe, the Quran was sent to restore.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings about this topic come up often enough to deserve direct answers.

First: "Worship in Islam means only the five daily prayers." Salah, the ritual prayer, is the most visible and structured act of worship in Islam, but ibadah is far broader, covering every sincere act of obedience to God, from fasting and charity to honesty in business and kindness to parents, as Quran 17:23 illustrates.

Second: "Christians believe they worship three separate gods." That is not the claim of mainstream Christian doctrine: the Trinity is presented as one God in three persons, not three gods, and Christians reject the charge of worshipping three gods as a misunderstanding of their belief.

Third: "Muslims do not honor or love Jesus." Muslims revere Jesus as one of the mightiest messengers God ever sent, believe in his virgin birth and miracles, and await his return before Judgment Day. What Muslims withhold from Jesus is worship specifically, not honor, love, or belief in him.

Fourth: "Worshipping Jesus is stated as plainly in the Bible as anything else in it." As the biblical-perspective section shows, the Bible contains a spectrum: some texts define worship as directed to God alone (John 4:24, Matthew 4:10), while others are read by Christians as extending worship to Jesus (John 20:28, Philippians 2:10-11). A fair comparison recognizes that spectrum honestly.

Fifth: "The New Testament recording people bowing before Jesus settles the question." The Greek word often translated "worship" in Gospel scenes, proskyneo, was also used for bowing respectfully before a king or teacher, without any claim of deity. Context, not the word alone, determines whether a scene depicts formal worship of God or reverence for an honored human being.

sections.title: Conclusion: Worship as the Single Undivided Right of God
sections.body:
Worship, in the Islamic view, is both the simplest and the most consequential question a person can face: to whom do I bow my heart? The Quran's answer never wavers: God created every soul for this very purpose, and the whole of religious life, from the words recited in every prayer to kindness shown to aging parents, exists to express it.

What gives that answer its force is that it needs no later councils or centuries of doctrinal refinement to state it. "It is You we worship and You we ask for help," recited daily by well over a billion people, needs no footnote. Even within the Gospels, on the Islamic reading, the same simplicity surfaces in Jesus's own words to Satan: worship and service belong to the Lord God alone. Muslims see in that verse the same creed carried by every prophet before Muhammad, peace be upon them all, and understand Islam's mission as calling people back to it, not away from Jesus, but toward the God Jesus himself worshipped and served.

For the Muslim, this shapes daily life: every act, however small, becomes an opportunity for worship when done sincerely for God, and every temptation to place ultimate trust or reverence in any created being is a temptation to divide what cannot be divided. Islam's invitation is to receive Jesus as the Quran presents him, a beloved and honored messenger of God, and to direct toward God alone the worship that the Quran, and Jesus's own recorded words, reserve for Him.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What does the Arabic word ibadah actually mean?
A: Ibadah comes from a root meaning "to serve" or "to submit." It refers to any sincere word or deed, ritual or ordinary, done to please God, including prayer, charity, honesty, and kindness, so long as it is directed to God alone.

Q: Does Islam allow honoring prophets or righteous people at all?
A: Yes. Muslims are commanded to love, honor, and follow the example of the prophets, including Jesus, and to show deep respect to parents and teachers. What is reserved for God alone is worship itself: prayer, sacrifice, and ultimate submission.

Q: Do Christians believe they worship three gods?
A: No. Mainstream Christian doctrine teaches one God existing in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, and Christian theologians explicitly reject the charge of worshipping three separate gods.

Q: What is the strongest biblical text Christians point to for worshipping Jesus?
A: Two texts are usually cited together: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God!" in John 20:28, and Paul's statement in Philippians 2:10-11 that every knee will bow and every tongue confess Jesus as Lord, language that echoes Isaiah's description of worship owed to God alone.

Q: How does Islam respond to those texts?
A: Muslims answer both halves of Thomas's exclamation, not only "my Lord." "My Lord" has precedent as a human honorific in Semitic speech; "my God," the harder half, does not have that same easy secular parallel, so Muslims read the whole sentence as a single exclamation, an astonished invocation of God's own name and power at witnessing what Thomas took to be God's act of raising Jesus from the dead, rather than a claim that the man in front of him was literally God, noting also that the text records no bowing or formal rite of worship following the words. On Paul's hymn, Muslims note that the passage ends by giving glory "to God the Father," describing Jesus as exalted and given his name by God rather than as an independent object of worship. Muslims also point to Matthew 4:10, where Jesus states directly that worship and service belong to God alone.

Q: What is shirk, and how does it relate to worship?
A: Shirk means associating partners with God, including directing any act of worship, such as prayer or ultimate devotion, toward a created being. Because worship is defined in Islam as God's exclusive right, shirk is considered the gravest of all wrongs.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following well-known works are offered as further reading for readers who want to go deeper. They are listed for orientation, not as sources for specific claims made in this article.

Larry W. Hurtado — Lord Jesus Christ: Devotion to Jesus in Earliest Christianity: a major academic study tracing how devotion to Jesus developed in the earliest Christian communities.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God: The Exaltation of a Jewish Preacher from Galilee: a historical-critical account, from a secular New Testament scholar, of how beliefs about Jesus's divine status developed over time.

Abu Ameenah Bilal Philips — The Fundamentals of Tawheed (Islamic Monotheism): a widely read English-language introduction to the Islamic doctrine of exclusive worship and the forms of shirk.

Ibn Taymiyyah (translated) — Al-'Ubudiyyah: Being a True Slave of Allah: a classical Islamic treatise devoted specifically to the comprehensive meaning of worship (ibadah) in Islam.

Satan! For it is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.'"
